sentences.base_language: It was Junseo's thirty-fifth birthday and his friends had been secretly planning a surprise party for weeks.
sentences.target_language: 준서의 서른다섯 번째 생일이었고, 친구들은 몇 주째 몰래 깜짝 파티를 준비하고 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Junseo-eui seoreundaseot beonjjae saengil-ieotgo, chingudeul-eun myeot jujjae mollae kkamjjak pati-reul junbihago isseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고 있다** progressive: 준비하**고 있었다** (had been preparing). **-고 있다** describes an ongoing action; in past narrative **-고 있었다** sets a background action that was in progress over an extended period. With the time expression 몇 주째 (for weeks) it emphasises the sustained, continuous nature of the planning: 공부하**고 있었다** (had been studying), 기다리**고 있었다** (had been waiting).
**몇 주째** duration with **-째**: **몇 주째** (for weeks now / for the nth week). The suffix **-째** attached to a counter expresses 'for [this many] units continuously': 사흘**째** (for three days running), 두 달**째** (for two months now). It carries the nuance that the situation has been ongoing without interruption up to the present moment.

sentences.base_language: He spent the morning thinking it would be a quiet day — he had told everyone he didn't want any fuss.
sentences.target_language: 준서는 조용한 하루가 될 거라고 생각하면서 오전을 보냈다. 소란 피우지 말라고 모두에게 미리 말해 뒀던 것이다.
sentences.romanized: Junseo-neun joyonghan haru-ga doel georago saenggakhamyeonseo ojeon-eul bonaetda. Soran piuji mallago modueege miri malhaedwotdeon geot-ida.
literal: that it would be a quiet day (quoted thought)
telling [them] not to make a fuss (quoted command)
it was that [he] had told (and left it in place).
sentences.grammarNote:
**-면서** simultaneous manner: 생각하**면서** 오전을 보냈다 (spent the morning while thinking). **-면서** shows two actions by the same subject happening at the same time: 음악을 들으**면서** 걸었다 (walked while listening to music). Here it paints the morning as coloured entirely by that mistaken assumption — he went through the hours wrapped in the thought that nothing would happen.
**-아/어 두다** + **-았/었던 것이다** preparatory past explanation: 말해 **뒀던 것이다** (it was that he had told [them] in advance and left it at that). **-아/어 두다** signals an action done in advance whose result persists: 써 **두다** (write down in advance), 사 **두다** (buy ahead). Combined with **-았/었던 것이다** the sentence retrospectively explains why he expected a quiet day.

sentences.base_language: His closest friend called in the afternoon and casually suggested coming over for a simple dinner.
sentences.target_language: 오후에 가장 친한 친구가 전화를 걸어 와서, 간단한 저녁 식사나 하러 오라고 아무렇지 않은 듯 제안했다.
sentences.romanized: Ohu-e gajang chinhan chingu-ga jeonhwa-reul georeo waseo, gandanhan jeonyeok siksa-na hareo orago amureoji anheun deut jaenanhaetda.
literal: to come for a simple dinner or so (quoted suggestion)
sentences.grammarNote:
**전화를 걸어 오다** compound motion: 전화를 **걸어 왔다** (called — dialed and it came [to him]). Korean telephone expressions use **걸다** (to hang/dial) + **오다/가다** for direction: 전화를 **걸어 오다** (call toward the speaker), 전화를 **걸어 가다** (call away from the speaker). This directional nuance, absent in English, is a natural feature of Korean motion-verb compounds.
**-나 하다** softening particle in embedded clause: 저녁 식사**나** 하러 오라고 (suggested coming over for a simple dinner or something). **-나** attached to a noun softens the suggestion, implying 'something like' or 'just a': 차**나** 한잔 해요 (let's just have a cup of tea or something). It is a key tool for making invitations sound casual and low-pressure — exactly what the friend needed to avoid arousing suspicion.

sentences.base_language: When Junseo arrived and the door opened, a room full of people shouted "Surprise!" all at once.
sentences.target_language: 준서가 도착해서 문이 열리자, 가득 찬 사람들이 일제히 「서프라이즈!」하고 외쳤다.
sentences.romanized: Junseo-ga dochakaeseo mun-i yeollija, gadeuk chan saramdeul-i iljjehi 「seopeulaijeu!」hago oechyeotda.
literal: as soon as the door opened,
sentences.grammarNote:
**-자** immediate sequential: 열리**자** 외쳤다 (the moment the door opened, they shouted). **-자** marks an action that follows immediately upon completion of the first: 앉**자** 시작했다 (the moment [he] sat down, [it] started). It is slightly more literary than **-자마자** but equally immediate; in narrative it captures the split-second trigger of the reveal — the door opened and, in that instant, the shout erupted.
**-하고** quotative for exclamations: 「서프라이즈!」**하고** 외쳤다 (shouted 「Surprise!」). **-하고** is used after quoted speech or sounds, particularly exclamations and onomatopoeia, before verbs of saying or sound: 「아이고!」**하고** 놀랐다 (startled, going 「Aigoo!」). It is more vivid and colloquial than the standard **-라고** quotative and is natural for capturing the spontaneous shout of a crowd.

sentences.base_language: His heart leaped and tears came to his eyes — he had had absolutely no idea.
sentences.target_language: 심장이 쿵 내려앉았고 눈물이 차올랐다. 전혀 눈치채지 못했던 것이다.
sentences.romanized: Simjang-i kung naeryeoanjatgo nunmul-i chaollatda. Jeonhyeo nunchijaeji motaetdeon geot-ida.
literal: the heart sank with a thud-and
it was that [he] had not noticed/suspected at all.
sentences.grammarNote:
**쿵** onomatopoeia as adverb: 심장이 **쿵** 내려앉았다 (the heart dropped with a thud). Korean makes abundant use of sound-symbolic words (의성어/의태어) placed directly before verbs: 가슴이 **두근** 거렸다 (the heart went thump-thump), 머리가 **띵** 했다 (the head went dizzy with a clang). **쿵** evokes the physical shock of the moment — a single syllable doing the work of a whole clause in English.
**눈치채다** = to notice, to pick up on: 눈치채**지 못했던 것이다** (it was that [he] had not caught on at all). **눈치** (social awareness, 'reading the room') + **채다** (to seize/catch): **눈치채다** means to notice something subtle that was not meant to be noticed. **-지 못하다** expresses inability or failure: 눈치채**지 못했다** (failed to pick up on it). The retrospective **-았/었던 것이다** frames this as the explanation for his overwhelming surprise.

sentences.base_language: The living room was decorated with balloons and colourful lights, and his favourite music was already playing.
sentences.target_language: 거실은 풍선과 알록달록한 조명으로 꾸며져 있었고, 좋아하는 음악이 이미 흘러나오고 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Geosil-eun pungseon-gwa allokdallokhan jomyeong-euro kkumyeojyeo isseotgo, joahaneun eumak-i imi heulleonaogo isseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어져 있다** passive resultant state: 꾸며져 **있었고** (was in a decorated state). **꾸미다** → **꾸며지다** (passive) + **-어 있다** (resultant state): the room had been decorated and remained so. Compare: **꾸며졌다** (was decorated — event), **꾸며져 있었다** (was in a decorated state — ongoing result). This distinction is crucial for describing scenes in Korean narrative.
**흘러나오다** = to flow/stream out: 음악이 **흘러나오고 있었다** (music was streaming out). **흐르다** (to flow) + **나오다** (to come out): a compound verb describing sound, liquid, or light emerging continuously from a source. The progressive **-고 있었다** emphasises that the music was already in full flow when he arrived — part of the prepared scene that greeted him.

sentences.base_language: Friends from every stage of his life were there — old classmates, colleagues, relatives he hadn't seen in years.
sentences.target_language: 인생의 모든 시기에서 온 친구들이 모여 있었다. 오래된 동창, 직장 동료, 몇 년째 못 봤던 친척들까지.
sentences.romanized: Insaeng-eui modeun sigi-eseo on chingudeul-i moyeo isseotda. Oraedoen dongchang, jikjang dongnyo, myeot nyeonjjae mot bwatdeon chincheokkdeulkkaji.
literal: who had come from every stage of life (past modifier)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄴ** past modifier + **에서 오다**: 인생의 모든 시기**에서 온** 친구들 (friends who came from every stage of life). **에서** marks the source or origin; **-(으)ㄴ** as a past modifier on **오다** creates 'who came from'. This stack of modifiers builds a rich noun phrase in a single breath — characteristic of Korean's head-final structure where modifiers always precede the noun.
**-까지** inclusive 'even': 친척들**까지** (even relatives). **-까지** extends the list to its most surprising member: 모르는 사람**까지** (even strangers). Here it caps the enumeration — classmates, colleagues, and, remarkably, even relatives he hadn't seen for years — conveying the comprehensiveness of the gathering. **몇 년째 못 봤던** uses **-던** (retrospective) to emphasise the long lapse since he had last seen them.

sentences.base_language: Junseo was overwhelmed with gratitude and went around the room hugging everyone and thanking them.
sentences.target_language: 준서는 감사함에 압도되어 방 안을 돌아다니면서 모두를 끌어안고 고마움을 전했다.
sentences.romanized: Junseo-neun gamsaham-e apdodoeeo bang an-eul doradanimyeonseo modeul-eul kkeureoango gomaum-eul jeonhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-면서** simultaneous action: 돌아다니**면서** 끌어안고 전했다 (went around while hugging and thanking). **-면서** here chains three concurrent actions — circling the room, embracing, thanking — into a single flowing image of Junseo moving through the party. The subject is the same throughout, and each action enriches the picture of his emotional response.
**끌어안다** = to pull into a hug, to embrace: **끌어안고** 고마움을 전했다 (hugged and conveyed thanks). **끌다** (to pull) + **안다** (to hold/embrace): a compound verb more physical and warm than simply **안다** alone — it implies drawing the person close. **고마움을 전하다** (to convey one's gratitude) is more deliberate and heartfelt than 감사하다: **마음을 전하다** (to convey one's feelings).

sentences.base_language: The table was covered with his favourite dishes, including a japchae dish his mother used to make.
sentences.target_language: 테이블은 그가 좋아하는 음식들로 가득 차 있었는데, 어머니가 늘 해 주시던 잡채도 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Teibeul-eun geu-ga joahaneun eumsikdeul-lo gadeuk cha isseotneunde, eomeoni-ga neul haejusideon japchae-do isseotda.
literal: that his mother always used to make (for him) (retrospective)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 주시다** honorific benefactive: 해 **주시던** (used to make [for him] — honorific). **-아/어 주다** (do as a favour for someone) + **-시-** (subject-honorific): **해 주시다** shows both that the mother did it for his benefit and that she is spoken of with respect. The retrospective **-던** further marks this as a recurring past action no longer happening — she used to make it; the dish now carries that emotional weight.
**-던** retrospective habitual modifier: 해 주시**던** 잡채 (the japchae [she] used to make). **-던** on a verb creates a modifier meaning 'that [subject] habitually did in the past': 살**던** 집 (the house [I] used to live in), 다니**던** 학교 (the school [I] used to attend). It differs from the simple past modifier **-(으)ㄴ** by emphasising the repeated, habitual nature of the past action and implying it no longer occurs.

sentences.base_language: Someone poured glasses of sparkling wine and everyone raised a toast to Junseo's health and happiness.
sentences.target_language: 누군가 스파클링 와인을 잔에 따르자, 모두가 준서의 건강과 행복을 위해 잔을 들었다.
sentences.romanized: Nugungga seupakeulling wain-eul jan-e ttareuja, moduga Junseo-eui geongang-gwa haengbok-eul wihae jan-eul deureotda.
literal: as soon as [they] poured sparkling wine into glasses,
sentences.grammarNote:
**-자** immediate sequential: 따르**자** 잔을 들었다 (the moment [someone] poured, everyone raised their glasses). **-자** captures the near-instantaneous response: one action triggers the next with no gap. It is slightly more formal than **-자마자** and fits the ceremonial, narrative tone of the toast scene: 문이 열리**자** 들어왔다 (the moment the door opened, [they] came in).
**잔을 들다** = to raise a glass (idiom): **잔을 들었다** (raised their glasses). **들다** (to lift/hold) + **잔** (glass/cup): this set phrase is the standard Korean expression for making a toast. Compare: **건배하다** (to make a toast — the act), **잔을 들다** (to raise the glass — the physical gesture). The phrase neatly captures the collective ritual of the moment.

sentences.base_language: Junseo gave a short, heartfelt speech and tried to express how much everyone in the room meant to him.
sentences.target_language: 준서는 짧지만 진심 어린 말을 전하면서, 그 자리에 있는 모든 사람이 자신에게 얼마나 소중한지 표현하려고 했다.
sentences.romanized: Junseo-neun jjalbji-man jinsim eorin mal-eul jeonhamyeonseo, geu jari-e inneun modeun saram-i jasin-ege eolmana sojunghanji pyohyeonharyogo haetda.
literal: while conveying short but heartfelt words-while,
everyone who was in that place-SUBJECT
how precious they were to him (indirect question)
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)려고 하다** attempt/intention: 표현하**려고 했다** (tried to express). **-(으)려고 하다** expresses intention or attempt, often with the nuance that full success is uncertain: 말하**려고 했지만** 못 했다 (tried to say but couldn't). Here it captures the struggle of putting deep emotion into words — he attempted to express it, implying the feeling was larger than language.
**-은지/는지** embedded indirect question: 얼마나 소중하**ㄴ지** (how precious [they were]). **-은지/는지** nominalises a question clause, embedding it as the object of a verb: 왜 왔**는지** 모른다 (don't know why [they] came), 어디 있**는지** 물었다 (asked where [it] was). The particle **얼마나** (how much) + **-은지** together build the indirect question 'how much… [they meant]'.

sentences.base_language: As the evening went on, someone turned up the music and the living room became an impromptu dance floor.
sentences.target_language: 저녁이 깊어 가면서, 누군가 음악 소리를 높이자 거실이 즉흥 댄스 플로어가 되어 버렸다.
sentences.romanized: Jeonyeok-i gipeo gamyeonseo, nugungga eumak sori-reul nopiija geosil-i jeukheung daenseu peulloeoga doeeo beoryeotda.
literal: as soon as someone turned up the music
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어 가다** progressive direction: 깊어 **가면서** (as [the evening] deepened). **-아/어 가다** attached to a process verb shows gradual change moving away from a starting point toward a result: 날이 밝아 **간다** (the day is growing bright), 시간이 흘러 **간다** (time flows on). Combined with **-면서** it creates the sense of a continuous arc — as the night was unfolding.
**-아/어 버리다** completion with finality: 되어 **버렸다** (became — and that was that). **-아/어 버리다** marks an action as completed fully and irreversibly, often with a sense of surprise or resignation: 먹어 **버렸다** (ate it all up), 가 **버렸다** (went and didn't come back). Here it captures the spontaneous, unstoppable transformation of the living room — before anyone quite decided it, the dance floor simply came into being.

sentences.base_language: His feet and back began to ache from dancing but he couldn't bring himself to stop.
sentences.target_language: 발과 허리가 춤을 추다가 아파 오기 시작했지만, 그만두려야 그만둘 수가 없었다.
sentences.romanized: Bal-gwa heori-ga chum-eul chudaga apa ogi sijakhaetjiman, geumandeuryeoya geumanduel su-ga eopseotda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-다가** transition mid-action: 춤을 추**다가** 아파 오기 시작했다 (was dancing and in the midst of that started to ache). **-다가** marks a situation where the first action was ongoing when a change occurred: 읽**다가** 잠들었다 (was reading and [then] fell asleep). It locates the onset of the ache inside the continuous flow of dancing.
**-(으)려야 -(으)ㄹ 수 없다** emphatic inability: 그만두**려야** 그만둘 수가 **없었다** (try as he might to stop, he could not). This construction combines the intention marker **-(으)려** with **-야** (even if) and **-(으)ㄹ 수 없다** (cannot): even the attempt or intention to do X is futile. It is a vivid idiom for expressing something one is powerless to resist: 잊**으려야** 잊을 수가 없었다 (try as I might to forget, I could not).

sentences.base_language: When the birthday cake appeared — a beautiful cake blazing with candles — everyone began to sing.
sentences.target_language: 생일 케이크가 등장했을 때, 촛불이 환하게 타오르는 아름다운 케이크를 보자 모두가 노래를 부르기 시작했다.
sentences.romanized: Saengil keikeu-ga deungjanghaesseul ttae, chossbul-i hwanhage taooreuneun areumdaun keikeu-reul boja moduga norae-reul bureogi sijakhaetda.
literal: as soon as [they] saw the beautiful cake
sentences.grammarNote:
**-았/었을 때** specific past moment: 등장**했을 때** (when [it] appeared). The past-tense modifier **-았/었을** in **-았/었을 때** pins the clause to a specific completed moment in the past — distinct from **-(으)ㄹ 때** (general or future) or **-는 때** (habitual). It sets the narrative scene precisely: 'at the moment the cake appeared'.
**타오르다** = to blaze up, to flame: 촛불이 **타오르는** 케이크 (a cake with candles blazing). **타다** (to burn) + **오르다** (to rise): a compound verb that evokes upward, vigorous burning. As a present modifier **타오르는** it vividly describes the sight of many candles burning brightly, creating a warm, festive image far more evocative than simply 켜진 (lit).

sentences.base_language: Junseo closed his eyes, made a silent wish, and blew out all the candles in one breath.
sentences.target_language: 준서는 눈을 감은 채 조용히 소원을 빌고 나서, 한 번에 촛불을 모두 불어 껐다.
sentences.romanized: Junseo-neun nun-eul gam-eun chae joyonghi sowon-eul bilgo naseo, han beon-e chossbul-eul modu bureo kkyeotda.
literal: after quietly making a wish-and then,
in one breath / all at once
sentences.grammarNote:
**-(으)ㄴ 채** maintained state: 눈을 감**은 채** 소원을 빌었다 (made a wish with his eyes remaining closed). **-(으)ㄴ 채** (sometimes **-(으)ㄴ 채로**) describes a secondary state that is maintained throughout the main action — the eyes closed and stayed closed while he wished. Compare: **-면서** (simultaneous action by the same subject), **-(으)ㄴ 채** (a resultant state that persists): 옷을 입**은 채** 잠들었다 (fell asleep with clothes on).
**-고 나서** completion before next action: 소원을 빌**고 나서** 껐다 (after finishing the wish, blew out [the candles]). **-고 나서** emphasises that the first action was fully completed before the second began: 밥을 먹**고 나서** 나갔다 (went out after finishing eating). It adds a sense of deliberate sequencing — the wish came first, whole and complete, and only then did he blow.

sentences.base_language: The cake was extraordinary — soft and moist, with a cream that reminded him of childhood.
sentences.target_language: 케이크는 정말 특별했다. 부드럽고 촉촉했으며, 크림은 어린 시절을 떠올리게 했다.
sentences.romanized: Keikeu-neun jeongmal teukbyeolhaetda. Budeureopgo chokcokhaetseumyeo, keurim-eun eorin sijeol-eul tteoollige haetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-게 하다** causative: 어린 시절을 떠올리**게 했다** (made [him] recall childhood). **-게 하다** forms a causative construction with any verb or adjective: 웃**게 하다** (make laugh), 슬프**게 하다** (make sad). Here the cream is the subject causing the recall — a gentle, indirect causative that is more evocative than simply 생각나게 했다 (made [him] think of). It places the agency on the sensation itself.
**촉촉하다** = to be moist, soft and slightly damp (in a pleasant way): **촉촉했으며** (was moist-and). **촉촉** is a mimetic word (의태어) describing a pleasingly moist texture — common in food description for cakes, skin, and fabric. **-으며** is a formal written connector (equivalent to **-고**) linking adjectives in a list: 맑**으며** 아름답다 (is clear and beautiful). Its slightly literary register suits the appreciative, reflective tone here.

sentences.base_language: Long after midnight, the last guests began saying their goodbyes with warm embraces at the door.
sentences.target_language: 자정이 한참 지나서야 마지막 손님들이 문 앞에서 따뜻하게 포옹하며 작별 인사를 하기 시작했다.
sentences.romanized: Jajeong-i hancham jinaseo-ya majimak sonimdeul-i mun ap-eseo ttatteutage poong-hamyeo jabbyeol insa-reul hagi sijakhaetda.
literal: only after midnight had long passed
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어서야** delayed realisation / 'only after': 한참 지나**서야** (only after it had long passed). **-아/어서야** combines **-아/어서** (causal/temporal) with **-야** (only then/only when): 어두워**져서야** 집에 갔다 (only went home when it had grown dark). It implies that the event was later than expected or that a condition had to be fully met before anything happened — midnight had long since passed before the first goodbye.
**-며** simultaneous action (written/formal): 포옹하**며** 인사했다 (said goodbye while embracing). **-며** is the literary/formal equivalent of **-면서**, connecting two simultaneous actions by the same subject. It is common in written narrative and formal prose: 미소를 지으**며** 말했다 (spoke while smiling). Its formal register suits the warm but slightly ceremonial quality of the final farewells.

sentences.base_language: When everyone had finally left, Junseo stood alone in the quiet room, still glowing with happiness.
sentences.target_language: 마침내 모두가 떠나고 나서, 준서는 고요한 방 안에 혼자 서서 여전히 행복감에 빛나고 있었다.
sentences.romanized: Machimae moduga tteonago naseo, Junseo-neun goyohan bang an-e honja seoseo yeojeonhi haengbokgam-e bitnago isseotda.
literal: after everyone had finally left-and then,
sentences.grammarNote:
**-고 나서** completion before next action: 떠나**고 나서** 서 있었다 (stood [there] after [everyone] had left). **-고 나서** emphasises full completion of the first action before the second: 끝나**고 나서야** 알았다 (only realised after it had fully ended). Here it marks the clean transition — once the last guest was gone, only then did Junseo's quiet moment begin.
**빛나다** = to shine, to glow: 행복감에 **빛나고 있었다** (was glowing with happiness). **빛나다** (to shine/glow) used figuratively to describe a person radiating an inner emotion is a common Korean literary device: 눈이 **빛났다** (eyes shone), 얼굴이 **빛나다** (the face glows). The progressive **-고 있었다** sustains the glow into the quiet aftermath, showing the happiness was still present and active.

sentences.base_language: He sent a long voice message to his closest friend, thanking her for organizing everything.
sentences.target_language: 준서는 가장 친한 친구에게 긴 음성 메시지를 보내서, 모든 것을 준비해 줘서 고맙다고 전했다.
sentences.romanized: Junseo-neun gajang chinhan chingu-ege gin eumseong mesiji-reul bonaeseo, modeun geot-eul junbihae jwoseo gomapda-go jeonhaetda.
literal: sent a long voice message-and so,
sentences.grammarNote:
**-아/어서** cause within quoted content: 준비해 **줘서** 고맙다고 (that [he] was grateful because [she] had prepared). **-아/어서** links cause and effect; here it is embedded inside the reported speech: 도와**줘서** 감사하다고 했다 (said that [he] was grateful because [she] helped). The causal chain — she organised, therefore he is grateful — is packed efficiently into a single quotative clause.
**-아/어 주다** benefactive: 준비해 **줘서** (because [she] prepared [it] for [him]). **-아/어 주다** marks that the action was performed for the speaker's (or a third party's) benefit: 가르쳐 **주다** (teach for someone), 만들어 **주다** (make for someone). The implied direction — she did the work so that he could enjoy it — is precisely what he is now thanking her for.

sentences.base_language: Lying in bed that night, he thought this was exactly the kind of evening that stayed with you for the rest of your life.
sentences.target_language: 그날 밤 침대에 누운 채, 준서는 이런 저녁이야말로 평생 마음속에 남게 되는 종류의 것이라고 생각했다.
sentences.romanized: Geunal bam chimdae-e nuun chae, Junseo-neun ireon jeonyeog-iyamallo pyeongsaeng maeumso-ge namge doeneun jongyu-eui geot-irago saenggakhaetda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**-게 되다** natural transition / coming to be: 남**게 되는** 것 (the kind of thing that comes to stay). **-게 되다** expresses a change of state or the natural, inevitable result of circumstances — not a deliberate act but something that just happens: 알**게 됐다** (came to know), 좋아하**게 됐다** (came to like). Here it captures the organic way certain memories embed themselves in the heart — not chosen, just inevitable.
**-이야말로** emphatic 'precisely, truly': 이런 저녁**이야말로** (evenings like this, precisely / it is exactly evenings like this that). **-이야말로** (after consonant) / **-야말로** (after vowel) is an emphatic topic marker that singles out the noun as the quintessential example: 이것**이야말로** 진짜다 (this, precisely, is the real thing). It gives the final sentence its sense of quiet conviction — this was exactly, undeniably, that kind of evening.
